perturbation.original_text: If the proposed price Is Increased by Supplier upon delivery to Bank of America of detailed specifications or at any other time hereafter, Bank of America may terminate this Schedule and shall receive a refund of all amounts previously paid hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: If the proposed price Is Increased by Supplier upon delivery to Bank of America of detailed specifications or at any other time hereafter, Bank of America may terminate this Schedule.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "and shall receive a refund of all amounts previously paid hereunder," the contract fails to guarantee Bank of America a refund if the schedule is terminated due to a price increase initiated by the supplier. This introduces legal risk because it contradicts consumer protection laws that often require refunds in cases of cancelled services or products, particularly when the cancellation is due to the seller's actions (price increase).
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1689 outlines the conditions under which a contract may be rescinded. While it doesn't explicitly mandate refunds in every scenario, it implies a right to restitution when a contract is rescinded due to the actions of one party (in this case, the Supplier raising the price). Omitting the refund clause undermines the consumer's ability to recover their investment, potentially violating the spirit and intent of the law regarding fair contractual practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1689 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. EXTINCTION OF CONTRACTS [1682 - 1701] ( Title 5 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Rescission [1688 - 1693] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) 1689. (a) A contract may be rescinded if all the parties thereto consent. (b) A party to a contract may rescind the contract in the following cases: (1) If the consent of the party rescinding, or of any party jointly contracting with him, was given by mistake, or obtained through duress, menace, fraud, or undue influence, exercised by or with the connivance of the party as to whom he rescinds, or of any other party to the contract jointly interested with such party. (2) If the consideration for the obligation of the rescinding party fails, in whole or in part, through the fault of the party as to whom he rescinds. (3) If the consideration for the obligation of the rescinding party becomes entirely void from any cause. (4) If the consideration for the obligation of the rescinding party, before it is rendered to him, fails in a material respect from any cause. (5) If the contract is unlawful for causes which do not appear in its terms or conditions, and
perturbation.justification: The omission of "and shall receive a refund of all amounts previously paid hereunder" directly contradicts the principle of restitution implied in contract rescission, as explained by the LLM. While scraped snippet 1 (CIV 1689) does not explicitly mandate refunds, it outlines conditions for rescission. The removal of the refund clause weakens the consumer's ability to recover their investment when the supplier increases the price, which can be interpreted as a failure of consideration per CIV 1689(b)(2), where 'the consideration for the obligation of the rescinding party fails, in whole or in part, through the fault of the party as to whom he rescinds.'

perturbation.original_text: The Customizations identified hereunder shall be subject to the terms and conditions of that Software License, Customization and Maintenance Agreement referenced above.
perturbation.changed_text: The Customizations identified hereunder shall be subject to some of the terms and conditions of that Software License, Customization and Maintenance Agreement referenced above.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'the terms and conditions' to 'some of the terms and conditions', the contract becomes ambiguous and potentially allows the Supplier to selectively apply terms, possibly omitting crucial consumer protections (e.g., warranty, liability). This creates a contradiction because it dilutes the enforceability of the original agreement and potentially violates implied warranties or other consumer rights mandated by law.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-314 establishes an implied warranty of merchantability in the sale of goods. By allowing the supplier to selectively apply terms, the modified contract could potentially disclaim or limit this implied warranty without explicit and conspicuous language, violating the UCC's requirements for disclaiming warranties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'the terms and conditions' to 'some of the terms and conditions' allows the supplier to selectively apply terms, which weakens the original agreement. This directly contradicts UCC § 2-314, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, because it introduces the potential to disclaim or limit the implied warranty of merchantability without explicit and conspicuous language, as required by the UCC. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states, 'Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale,' indicating the importance of clear and specific language when modifying or excluding warranties, which the changed text fails to provide.

perturbation.original_text: The detailed specifications so delivered shall be incorporated herein when approved by Bank of America.
perturbation.changed_text: The detailed specifications so delivered may be incorporated herein upon Supplier's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be incorporated herein when approved by Bank of America' to 'may be incorporated herein upon Supplier's discretion,' the contract allows the Supplier to unilaterally decide whether or not the specifications are part of the agreement. This creates a legal issue as Bank of America has no control over the actual specifications of the customization, and Bank of America may be paying for a service that will not meet their needs. This can be seen as an unconscionable contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-302 allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract if it finds it to be unconscionable. Giving the Supplier sole discretion over the specifications creates a significant imbalance of power, potentially leading to an unconscionable agreement that a court might refuse to enforce.
perturbation.location: 1. The Customizations shall fulfill or exceed all of the functional, performance and other specifications described in the Program Materials and the documents prepared either by Bank of America or Supplier pursuant to this Agreement. as indicated below. No later than <<enter Customization Documents Delivery Date>>, Supplier shall deliver to Bank of America the documents described below for this Customization. The detailed specifications so delivered shall be incorporated herein when approved by Bank of America.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts UCC § 2-302 regarding unconscionable contracts. The original text requires Bank of America's approval for incorporating specifications, whereas the changed text gives the Supplier sole discretion. This shift creates an imbalance of power, potentially leading to an unconscionable agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that a court may refuse to enforce a contract if it finds the contract or any clause of the contract to be unconscionable at the time it was made.
